notes: LNG through Hormuz has zero overland bypass — the oil model's escape-valve logic simply does not exist for gas, and there is no strategic LNG reserve anywhere. Shock model mirrors the oil one: multiplier ≈ 1 + (share of global LNG trade lost) ÷ ε_gas, banded and labeled illustrative, anchored to the 2021–22 European gas crisis.

hormuz_lng_mtpa.unit: Mt/yr LNG through Hormuz (Qatar ~77, UAE Das Island ~5.6)
hormuz_lng_mtpa.source: EIA WOTC; QatarEnergy / ADNOC public disclosures

global_lng_trade_mtpa.source: GIIGNL annual report; IEA gas market reports

bcfd_per_mtpa.unit: Bcf/d per Mt/yr (1 Mt LNG ≈ 48.6 Bcf)

eps_gas.unit: short-run gas demand elasticity band — tighter than oil; gas users have even fewer substitutes inside a season

mult_cap.source: Model bound — the 2022 crisis peaked near 10× before demand destruction

buyers_source: Approximate split of Qatari+UAE deliveries from GIIGNL / cargo-tracking commentary

anchor_2022.text: The empirical anchor: Russia's 2021–22 pipeline cuts removed roughly the gas-equivalent of 14% of globally traded LNG from Europe's supply — TTF peaked near 10× its pre-crisis level (≈€340/MWh in August 2022 vs ≈€35 in early 2021) before demand destruction bit.
anchor_2022.source: IEA gas market reports; public TTF price history

storage_note.text: There is no strategic reserve for gas. Europe's storage (~100 bcm, ~25% of annual demand) is a seasonal buffer measured in weeks of winter drawdown; Asian LNG buyers hold days, not months.
